top25Formulas.title: Magnitude of a vector
top25Formulas.trap: Do not add components directly. Magnitude of 2i + 3j is not 5; it is sqrt(13).

top25Formulas.title: Unit vector
top25Formulas.trap: Never divide by a zero vector because its direction is undefined.

top25Formulas.title: Vector addition
top25Formulas.trap: Do not add magnitudes unless the vectors are in the same direction.

top25Formulas.title: Vector subtraction
top25Formulas.trap: A - B and B - A are opposite vectors.

top25Formulas.title: Scalar multiplication
top25Formulas.latex: \lambda \vec A=\lambda a_1\hat i+\lambda a_2\hat j+\lambda a_3\hat k
top25Formulas.trap: If lambda = 0, the result is the zero vector with no direction.

top25Formulas.title: Section formula in vector form
top25Formulas.trap: For AP:PB = m:n, the formula is (mB + nA)/(m+n), not (mA + nB)/(m+n).

top25Formulas.title: Dot product geometry
top25Formulas.trap: A dot B = 0 means perpendicular only when both vectors are non-zero.

top25Formulas.title: Dot product component form
top25Formulas.trap: Do not multiply unlike components such as a1b2.

top25Formulas.title: Angle between two vectors
top25Formulas.trap: Use the angle between vectors, not angle from an axis unless one vector is that axis.

top25Formulas.title: Perpendicular condition
top25Formulas.trap: The zero vector has dot product zero with every vector but is not given a direction.

top25Formulas.title: Scalar projection
top25Formulas.trap: Scalar projection is a number; vector projection is a vector.

top25Formulas.title: Vector projection
top25Formulas.latex: \operatorname{proj}_{\vec B}\vec A=\frac{\vec A\cdot\vec B}{\lvert \vec B\rvert^2}\vec B
top25Formulas.trap: Denominator is |B|^2 because B itself is not usually a unit vector.

top25Formulas.title: Cross product magnitude
top25Formulas.trap: Cross product is zero for parallel vectors even if magnitudes are large.

top25Formulas.title: Cross product determinant
top25Formulas.trap: The j component has a negative sign during expansion.

top25Formulas.title: Parallel condition
top25Formulas.trap: Zero cross product may also involve a zero vector; check non-zero condition.

top25Formulas.title: Area of parallelogram
top25Formulas.trap: Do not use dot product for area.

top25Formulas.title: Area of triangle
top25Formulas.trap: Use two vectors from the same initial point.

top25Formulas.title: Direction cosines
top25Formulas.trap: Direction cosines are not the same as direction ratios unless the vector is already unit length.

top25Formulas.title: Direction cosine identity
top25Formulas.trap: Use squares. l + m + n is not generally one.

top25Formulas.title: Direction ratios
top25Formulas.latex: \text{For }\vec A=a\hat i+b\hat j+c\hat k,\text{ D.R.s are }a,b,c
top25Formulas.trap: Direction ratios are not unique.

top25Formulas.title: Scalar triple product
top25Formulas.latex: [\vec A\,\vec B\,\vec C]=\vec A\cdot(\vec B\times\vec C)
top25Formulas.trap: Changing order can change sign.

top25Formulas.title: Collinearity condition
top25Formulas.trap: Equal slopes in two components are not enough in 3D; check all components.

top25Formulas.title: Coplanarity condition
top25Formulas.trap: Coplanar does not mean pairwise parallel.

top25Formulas.title: Work as dot product
top25Formulas.trap: A large perpendicular force can do zero work.

top25Formulas.title: Torque as cross product
top25Formulas.trap: Force along the radius gives zero torque.

top20Concepts: Magnitude is length, direction is orientation.
Equal vectors can be located differently.
Vector addition combines effects, not just sizes.
Subtraction means adding the negative vector.
Scalar multiplication can reverse direction.
Unit vector is direction only.
Dot product gives a scalar along-effect.
cos theta appears because dot product measures projection.
Dot product zero means perpendicular for non-zero vectors.
Projection is a shadow.
Cross product gives a perpendicular vector.
sin theta appears because cross product measures area height.
A x B is the negative of B x A.
Cross product zero means parallel for non-zero vectors.
Area of parallelogram is |A x B|.
Area of triangle is half |A x B|.
Direction cosines are normalized components.
Collinearity uses scalar multiple form.
Coplanarity uses zero scalar triple product.
top15Traps: Adding vector magnitudes instead of components.
Forgetting zero vector has no definite direction.
Confusing scalar projection and vector projection.
Using cross product for perpendicular condition.
Missing the middle sign in determinant expansion.
Reversing A x B and B x A.
Using position vectors instead of side vectors for triangle area.
Forgetting non-zero condition in parallel/perpendicular tests.
Mixing AP:PB weights in section formula.
Not checking sign of dot product for angle type.
Normalizing when only direction ratios are asked.
Calling cross product magnitude a vector.
top10ThreeDIdeas: Axes with vector endpoint and dotted component box
Dot product angle and projection shadow
thirtyMinutePlan: 5 min: revise vector types and component form.
6 min: dot product formulas and projection.
6 min: cross product, area, and right-hand rule.
5 min: direction cosines and section formula.
lastDayPlan: Practice drawing triangle, parallelogram, and projection diagrams.
examHallStrategy: Identify whether the question asks length, shadow, area, direction, or condition.
Write vectors in component form immediately.
Keep exact radical form unless decimal is demanded.
finalChecklist: I can explain why vectors need magnitude and direction.
I can draw addition and subtraction diagrams.
I can derive magnitude, dot product, projection, cross product area, and direction cosine identity.
I can solve CBSE board-style steps neatly.
I can solve JEE Main component questions quickly.
I can explain right-hand rule and why A x B = -(B x A).
